narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of past students, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to forecast bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 30, middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and notably low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1—and predicted a 91% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this tool to prioritize admits likely to pass and succeed in practice. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your Year 3 decile ranking, your Year 1 decile ranking, and your undergraduate GPA, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 1, far below the typical student's 5.5-ish ranking, was the strongest push toward the failure prediction, as it signals consistently lower performance relative to peers at a critical stage. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1, again well under the average around 5.7, reinforced that concern by highlighting early academic struggles compared to most applicants. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10—slightly below the average of 3.21—actually nudged the prediction a bit toward passing, showing some resilience in college work. Overall, these early and mid-school rankings stood out as unusually low against typical students, overshadowing strengths like your full-time commitment, which is common among 93% of applicants. While your LSAT of 30 is also below the 37 average, it played a lesser role here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law amid real systemic challenges—many overcome similar starts—but the model flags patterns from past data where such rankings correlate with bar struggles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your growth. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools that value diverse perspectives like yours. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
